data.heading: My Contributions
items: Led UX redesign of the ARISE mobile AR experience across iOS and Android, refreshing UI assets for 5 minigames
Conducted user research sessions with child life specialists at partnering hospitals to validate design decisions
Created gameplay flow diagrams, wireframes, and high-fidelity mockups in Figma, and implemented final designs in Unity/C#

data.heading: In a Clamshell 🦀
body: ARISE is a mobile app that encourages pediatric patients to get up and move by sending them on an immersive undersea adventure. The app uses augmented reality to transform hospital hallways into an interactive ocean world where kids complete quests and collect virtual sea creatures.

I joined the ARISE team as the UX designer in charge of refreshing the app's beta interface. The existing UI was functional but cluttered, with low-contrast text, inconsistent navigation patterns, and visual noise that made it hard for young patients to focus on gameplay.

data.alt: ARISE app overview showing the refreshed UI
data.caption: Overview of the refreshed ARISE interface

data.heading: Objectives
body: 01 — Simplify navigation so young patients can play independently without adult assistance
02 — Increase visual contrast and readability for use in varying hospital lighting conditions
03 — Unify the visual language across all 5 minigames while preserving each game's unique character
The UI refresh touched every screen in the app. Below are before/after comparisons of the four major sections: Main Menu, Inventory, Quests, and Accounts.

before.label: Pre-Refresh
bullets: Cluttered navigation with competing visual elements
Low contrast text difficult to read in bright environments

after.label: Post-Refresh
bullets: Simplified navigation hierarchy with clear visual priority

before.label: Pre-Refresh
bullets: Dense grid layout overwhelming for young users
No clear visual feedback for collected vs. uncollected items

after.label: Post-Refresh
bullets: Cleaner grid with breathing room between items
Clear visual states for collected, uncollected, and locked creatures

before.label: Pre-Refresh
bullets: Quest objectives buried in small text
No progress indication for multi-step quests

after.label: Post-Refresh
bullets: Large, scannable quest cards with clear objectives
Visual progress bar and step indicators
Added contextual help screen for first-time players

before.label: Pre-Refresh
bullets: Complex account creation flow with too many steps
Avatar selection felt disconnected from the ocean theme

after.label: Post-Refresh
bullets: Sea creature avatar selection tied to gameplay progression

data.alt: Gameplay flow diagrams mapping the user journey through ARISE
data.caption: Flow diagrams mapping every screen and interaction path in the app

body: Before touching any visuals, I mapped the entire app's screen-to-screen flow. This revealed redundant navigation paths and dead-end states that were confusing young users. The diagrams became our shared reference for the rest of the project.

data.text: Due to patient privacy regulations (HIPAA), we were unable to conduct direct usability testing with pediatric patients. Instead, we validated our designs through interviews with child life specialists who work directly with patients daily.

images.alt: Interview session with child life specialist
images.caption: Child Life Specialist Interviews

images.alt: ARISE being playtested in a hospital setting
images.caption: In-context playtesting at a partner hospital

data.alt: Design workshop artifacts and collaborative sketching
data.caption: Collaborative design workshop with the SpellBound team

body: I facilitated design workshops with the SpellBound team to align on visual direction. We explored multiple aesthetic directions before converging on a clean, high-contrast style that maintained the playful ocean theme while improving usability.

images.caption: Early hand-drawn sketches exploring layout options
Low-fidelity wireframes for the main navigation flow

data.text: pediatric hospitals across the US using ARISE to improve the patient experience

links.label: Magic Portal
links.description: AR portal into an undersea world

data.heading: Wrapping Up
body: Working on ARISE was a formative experience — designing for children in hospitals requires a unique blend of playfulness and sensitivity. Every design decision had to account for kids who might be tired, anxious, or in pain.

The refreshed UI shipped in 2021 and is currently in use at over 20 pediatric hospitals. I'm grateful to the child life specialists who gave us their time and insight, and to the SpellBound team for trusting me with this redesign.
